title: Who needs a backflow preventer
description: A practical guide to the property types, hazard classes, and devices that usually trigger backflow assembly requirements.
intro: The answer is rarely just 'commercial' or 'residential.' Utilities care about whether a connection can contaminate the public water system and whether the hazard is low or high.
callout: Use this page to frame the risk. Then switch back to the utility page because the governing utility decides how the rule is enforced locally.
sourceExcerpt: Backflow device requirements follow hazard and contamination exposure, not just a simple residential-versus-commercial label, so the local utility must stay canonical.

sections.heading: High-hazard situations show up first
sections.body: Hospitals, restaurants, industrial facilities, commercial laundries, reclaimed water users, and other contamination-risk premises are common triggers for annual testing.
bullets: Many utilities treat pools with auto-fill and some irrigation setups as higher hazard than homeowners expect.
Restricted-inspection sites and premises with auxiliary water sources often get stronger isolation requirements.

sections.heading: Residential does not mean exempt
sections.body: Residential irrigation, pools, fire protection, wells, and some internal plumbing arrangements can still create a device requirement depending on local rules.
bullets: Some utilities split testing cadence between residential low-hazard irrigation and higher-hazard assemblies.
New construction and service activation can add customer service inspection or permit steps.
